title: Inside the Multilingual Transformer: Computational Neuroanatomy of Shared and Language-Specific Brain Alignment
abstract: I looked inside multilingual encoders and compared their internal signals against multilingual fMRI data. The interesting part: the shared cross-language alignment seems to come more from attention-side computations than the usual 'semantic FFN' story.
whyItMatters: A single benchmark score does not tell you what part of the model is doing the work. This gives multilingual model builders a more inspectable target.

title: Language-Neutral or Language-Specific? Disentangling Multilingual LLM Subspaces with Naturalistic fMRI
abstract: This paper separates shared multilingual structure from language-specific leftovers in XLM-R and NLLB-200 using naturalistic fMRI data. Less 'does it work?', more 'what kind of representation is it using?'.
whyItMatters: Useful when you want to audit whether a multilingual encoder is carrying meaning across languages or just getting good at language-specific residue.

title: Overlay Algebra: Causal Composition of Operational Behaviors in Frozen Language Models
abstract: A method for adding operational behaviors like strict JSON or exact quotation on top of a frozen model without retraining the whole thing. The goal is to make output contracts reusable and testable.
whyItMatters: Teams keep needing small, boring behavior guarantees from large models. This treats those guarantees as something you can isolate, ship, and audit.

title: Selective Revocation and Replay: Post-Compromise Recovery of Explicit Persisted State in Memory-Augmented LLM Agents
abstract: A recovery method for poisoned agent memory. Instead of wiping everything, it tracks where bad state came from, revokes descendants, and replays only the parts that need repair.
whyItMatters: Agent memory is useful until it carries attacker garbage forward. This gives operators a cleaner option than 'delete everything and hope'.

title: Same-Size Capability Transfer Reveals a Performance-Localization Tradeoff in Deterministic Function Calling
abstract: A locked testbed for moving function-calling behavior between same-family models. Sparse transfer worked, but the dense module still won the main metric, which is exactly the kind of inconvenient result worth knowing.
whyItMatters: It gives model-editing claims a deterministic check instead of a nice story and a few cherry-picked examples.

title: Surgical Post-Training Diffing: Partial Recovery Without Clean Small-Mask Separation
abstract: A paired diffing study of Gemma post-training. It recovered some capability signal, but the clean small-mask story did not really survive contact with held-out evaluation.
whyItMatters: A useful reminder that 'we found the circuit' and 'we found something that generalizes' are not the same sentence.

title: ProbeRoute: Probes as Routing Priors for Frozen-Backbone Multi-Token Prediction
abstract: A cheap probe pass is used to initialize routing for multi-token prediction on a frozen backbone. The aim is better speculative drafting without making the adaptation recipe bigger and messier.
whyItMatters: If you can get a better route from a small offline probe, you should not need to unfreeze half the model just to test the idea.

title: When Accuracy Hides Path Dependence: Criterion Fidelity in LLM Judges
abstract: This paper tests whether LLM judges follow the actual criterion or just react to how the criterion is worded. Accuracy can look fine while the judge is still path-dependent in a way you would not want in production.
whyItMatters: Good judge pipelines need to survive paraphrase and counterfactual checks. Otherwise the reviewer is grading the wording, not the work.

title: Binary Evidence Sufficiency Dissociation in Reasoning-Model Hidden States
abstract: A probe study asking whether reasoning models carry an internal signal for 'I have enough evidence now.' That signal appears separable from just getting the final answer right.
whyItMatters: For retrieval and agent systems, knowing when the model has enough evidence may matter more than another confident-looking answer.

title: Structured Yet Fragile: Signed Intervention Geometry of Matched ORF and CRISPR Cell Painting Profiles
abstract: A locked analysis of matched ORF and CRISPR Cell Painting profiles. The structure is real, but fragile, and the retrieval utility disappears under stricter checks.
whyItMatters: Negative results like this are useful. They tell you which biological claims still hold when analytic flexibility is taken away.

title: Outcome Is Not Verification: Auditing Hidden-State Verifiers with Counterfactual Local Validity
abstract: An audit of hidden-state verifiers that separates 'the final answer was right' from 'the process was locally valid.' Those are easy to blur and dangerous to confuse.
whyItMatters: If a verifier is just reading outcome signals, it should not be sold as process verification. This paper gives teams a way to check that.

title: First-Unsafe-Step Counterfactual DPO for KPI-Gaming in Autonomous LLM Agents: Held-Out Evaluation Under a Capability-Safety Bottleneck
abstract: A held-out agent-safety study that finds the first unsafe step, rewrites it safely, and trains on that contrast. The catch: preference training is limited by what the base model can even produce on-policy.
whyItMatters: It is a practical warning for agent safety work: better labels do not magically fix a weak behavioral horizon.

title: What Survives Control Calibration? A Full-Scope Negative Result for a Locked Minimum-Description Acceptance Criterion
abstract: A full-scope negative result for a locked causal-abstraction acceptance rule. Across the tested settings, no proposed abstraction class earned certification.
whyItMatters: This is the kind of failure report interpretability needs more of: clear rules, no post-hoc rescue, and boundaries on what can be claimed.

title: CacheMedic++: Robust KV-Cache Stabilization via Self-Distillation
abstract: A reproducible way to corrupt, diagnose, and repair KV-cache failures using distillation. The goal is to turn weird serving instability into something you can actually test.
whyItMatters: Serving failures are easier to fix when they are repeatable. This makes that repeatability part of the workflow.

title: CertiPatch: Specification Repair for Frozen Language Models with Replayable Empirical Certificates
abstract: A repair workflow for frozen models that finds counterexamples, patches the behavior, and leaves behind replayable certificates.
whyItMatters: Model repair should not be a vibes-based sign-off. This turns it into something teams can rerun and inspect.

title: ScrubID: Identifiability-Aware Auditing for Mechanistic Interpretability
abstract: An audit method for checking whether interpretability claims are actually identifiable from the evidence. Pretty plots are not enough.
whyItMatters: It raises the bar for interpretability claims by asking what can be independently checked.

title: Discover-Then-Distill: Separating Test-Time Adaptation from Self-Distillation Consolidation
abstract: A compute-matched pipeline that separates short-term adaptation from durable improvement. The boring question matters: did we improve the model, or just help it survive the test?
whyItMatters: It helps teams avoid mistaking temporary adaptation for a real tuning win.

title: AvalancheLLM: Token-Layer Activation Event Cascades in LLMs under Gain Scaling
abstract: A controlled study of activation cascades under gain changes. It looks for instability patterns in the model before they show up as weird behavior downstream.
whyItMatters: If a model becomes brittle under small scaling changes, you want to find out in a lab, not from users.

title: RIA: Retokenization Invariance Atlas
abstract: A deterministic audit for cases where semantically identical prompt formatting still changes the answer. Newline roulette is not a reliability strategy.
whyItMatters: It gives teams evidence about whether formatting changes are harmless or quietly changing outcomes.

title: CIS Technical Report
abstract: A practical reliability report for AI systems: deterministic checks, evidence gates, and implementation notes for teams that have to ship the thing, not just demo it.
whyItMatters: It turns the reliability philosophy into something a delivery team can actually adopt.
